planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; in zone 9 sow February–March for spring or September–October for fall. Prizehead is one of the fastest-maturing looseleaf lettuces and can be harvested as baby leaves in 25–30 days or at full size in 45–55 days. The quick maturity makes it excellent for succession planting every 2 weeks through the cool season.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Prizehead is a classic American heirloom looseleaf lettuce developed by W. Atlee Burpee Company in the 1880s and still widely grown for its exceptional flavor and striking red-bronze tipped leaves. The large, frilly, bright green leaves are broadly and dramatically tipped in red-bronze to burgundy at the leaf edges, creating a distinctly two-toned effect that makes Prizehead one of the most ornamental of all heirloom lettuces and an enduring favorite in kitchen gardens. The flavor is superb — mild, tender, and buttery with a crisp texture that surpasses many newer commercial varieties in taste trials — and the quick maturity (45–55 days to full size) allows it to be harvested before summer heat arrives. The frilly, slightly ruffled leaf texture holds dressings well. In zone 9 Prizehead is equally at home in spring and fall gardens; the red tips intensify in cooler weather and provide the most dramatic color in fall plantings.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Prizehead Lettuce Heritage Variety
W. Atlee Burpee Company — Classic American Heirloom Vegetables
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heat-Tolerant Heirloom Lettuces

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall harvest or January–February for spring. Red Oak Leaf is exceptionally heat-tolerant for a red lettuce and holds quality longer than most red types before bolting. Succession sow every 3 weeks through fall for continuous harvest. Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again or pull entire plants.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Red Oak Leaf is the red-leafed counterpart to the classic green Oak Leaf lettuce — producing deeply lobed, sinuate leaves with an outline resembling the characteristic deeply-indented silhouette of an oak leaf, in deep burgundy-red to bronze-red coloring. The deeply-indented, non-frilly leaf form distinguishes Oak Leaf types immediately from the more common looseleaf lettuces with wavy or ruffled edges. The flavor is mild, tender, and slightly nutty — one of the better-flavored looseleaf lettuces with a soft, non-bitter quality. Red Oak Leaf lettuce is notably heat-tolerant and slow-to-bolt compared to most red lettuces, making it particularly valuable for extending the spring harvest season in zone 9 and for reliable color retention in warm fall conditions when other red varieties revert to green. The striking leaf form and consistent deep-red color make it one of the most popular lettuces for mesclun mixes and specialty salad blends.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Red Oak Leaf Lettuce Heritage Variety
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Oak Leaf Lettuce Types
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Looseleaf Lettuce

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for fall harvest. Bianca Riccia is the standard Italian fine-cut white endive for winter production, producing a naturally pale center without tying. In zone 9 the cooler temperatures from October through December provide ideal growing conditions.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Bianca Riccia (White Curly) is an Italian fine-cut curly endive producing a loose, open rosette of very finely divided, heavily frizzy white-to-cream leaves — naturally self-blanching at the center without the tying required by some other frisee types. The fine cutting and natural pale color of the interior make Bianca Riccia one of the most elegant frisee types for Italian insalata recipes and for the French frisee au lardons preparation where the delicate pale center is the centerpiece. The outer leaves, being more exposed, are darker green and more bitter; the pale interior heart is the culinary prize with its mild, slightly bitter, elegant flavor. Unlike darker-centered frisee varieties, Bianca Riccia requires little or no artificial blanching because the dense, overlapping outer leaves naturally shade the center as the head matures. In zone 9 fall planting produces heads of excellent quality through November and December.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Bianca Riccia Endive Growing Notes
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Curly Endive Types
Territorial Seed Company — Italian Endive and Frisee

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 start July–August and transplant September–October for a December–March harvest. Celery germination is slow and requires light — sow on the surface and keep evenly moist at 60–65°F. In zone 9 celery is a winter crop; the Gulf Coast's mild winters allow consistent production from November through March without protection. Consistent irrigation is critical — celery is among the most water-demanding vegetables.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into very fertile, consistently moist, well-drained soil in full sun; Utah 52-70 is the standard commercial and home garden celery in North America — an improved Utah-type celery producing tall, upright stalks of thick, crisp, rounded (not hollow) pale-green to cream-colored ribs with a classic, full celery flavor. The '52-70' designation refers to the original development period. Utah-type celery has largely replaced older Pascal types in commercial production because of superior rib thickness, crunchiness, and reduced stringiness compared to older varieties. Celery is among the most demanding vegetables in terms of water and fertility requirements; it grows naturally in moist, fertile wetland soils and demands consistent irrigation and rich soil throughout its long growing period. Blanch by earthing up or wrapping paper around the stalks 2 weeks before harvest to lighten the color and reduce bitterness. In zone 9 celery is exclusively a cool-season vegetable, transplanted in fall and harvested through winter.
sources: Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Celery
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Celery Production for Florida
University of California Cooperative Extension — Celery Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; in zone 9 sow February–March for spring or September–October for fall. Sweet Fennel does not transplant well — always direct sow in place. Unlike Florence fennel (finocchio) which is grown for a swollen bulb base, Dulce Sweet Fennel is grown for seeds and leaf — the characteristic Italian feathery herb. Fennel is allelopathic (inhibits growth of many neighboring plants); site it away from tomatoes, peppers, and dill.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun — do not transplant; Dulce (Sweet) fennel is the culinary fennel grown for its fine, feathery, anise-flavored fronds and seeds — the herb fennel of Italian and Mediterranean cooking used in fish preparations, sausages, salads, and as a garnish, distinct from Florence fennel (finocchio) which forms the swollen bulb base. The tall, graceful, blue-green fronds have an intensely sweet anise-licorice flavor and fragrance; both fresh and dried fronds are used throughout Italian and Provençal cooking. The seeds (technically a double achene) are the primary flavoring in Italian sausage, Tuscan salumi, the spice mixes of southern French cooking, and liqueurs including pastis and absinthe. Dulce fennel is perennial in zone 9 and zone 10, returning from roots each spring; in colder zones it is grown as an annual. Site carefully as fennel inhibits the growth of many neighboring vegetables through allelopathic root exudates — keep it at least 3 feet from tomatoes, peppers, and beans.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Finocchio Dulce Growing Notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Fennel Herb Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Mediterranean Herbs for Texas Gardens
